title: Nine Years in Plain Sight: The "Copy Fail" Kernel Bug That Handed Root to Anyone Who Asked
summary: A performance optimization added to Linux's in-kernel AEAD crypto path in 2017 turns out to be a local privilege-escalation flaw — and after nine years in mainline, CISA has added CVE-2026-31431 ("Copy Fail") to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog amid active in-the-wild exploitation. The bug lets a local user corrupt a setuid binary inside the kernel's page cache and execute it as root, leaving the on-disk file untouched. Here's how it works, what's patched, and what distros and container operators should do now.
body:
Somewhere around 2017, a developer optimized Linux's in-kernel AEAD crypto path to work in-place. It was a sensible performance tweak. It was also, it turns out, wrong in a way nobody noticed for nine years — and now CISA has added it to the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog because people are actively using it to get root.

The bug lives in `crypto/algif_aead.c`, the kernel module that lets userspace programs access hardware-accelerated authenticated encryption via the AF_ALG socket interface. The 2017 change allowed the encrypt/decrypt operation to write output back into the same buffer it was reading from. Normal enough — except when "same buffer" means "different memory mappings of the same page," which is exactly what an attacker can arrange. The result: a controlled write of roughly four bytes into the kernel's page cache for any file the attacker can read. Four bytes into the right place is all you need.

Researchers at Theori (operating under the Xint brand) worked out that those four bytes could corrupt the in-memory copy of a setuid binary — `/usr/bin/su` is the canonical demo target — without touching anything on disk and without any race condition. The kernel's page cache is not the file. Corrupt the cache, execute the cached version, become root. Theori's public writeup, which CISA cites directly in the KEV entry's notes field, named the bug "Copy Fail." The name has stuck.

> **Researcher-attributed claims:** Theori/Xint describe a proof-of-concept involving a 732-byte script achieving root in seconds. These specifics have not been independently verified by this publication; treat them as researcher claims.

## The Nine-Year Comedy

There is a certain honesty in how this one aged. The patch that introduced the vulnerability landed in mainline in 2017. It sat in every kernel release since. Distributions shipped it, cloud providers ran it, containers inherited it. The in-place optimization saved a memcpy or two. It cost nine years of silent exploitability — and, now that exploitation is public, it costs a simultaneous scramble across every major distro's patch pipeline.

CISA added CVE-2026-31431 to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog on **May 1, 2026** with a FCEB remediation deadline of **May 15, 2026**. The CVSSv3.1 score is **7.8 HIGH** (AV:L/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H): local access, low privilege required, no user interaction. Once you have a shell, you have root.

The technical designation is CWE-669 (Incorrect Resource Transfer Between Spheres) — a classification that, in plain language, means "you wrote to memory you were supposed to be reading, and that turns out to matter."

## How the Flaw Works

Linux exposes cryptographic operations to userspace through the AF_ALG socket interface. Programs can request kernel-accelerated AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305, or other AEAD (Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data) ciphers without pulling those operations into userspace.

The 2017 optimization told `algif_aead` it was safe to write output in-place into the source buffer — a valid assumption if source and destination are the same physical allocation. The mistake: when an attacker constructs source and destination as *different userspace mappings of the same underlying page*, the kernel's aliasing check doesn't catch it. The in-place write proceeds. It corrupts the page cache entry for whatever file backs that page.

The attacker chooses a readable setuid file — `/usr/bin/su`, `/usr/bin/sudo`, or similar — maps its pages, runs the AEAD operation to overwrite a few bytes in the cached copy, then executes the file. The on-disk binary is untouched. The on-disk checksum passes. The kernel runs the attacker's version. Root.

## Patch Status (Snapshot — Verify Your Distro)

This is a moving target. As of late April 2026 reporting: Debian, Ubuntu, AlmaLinux, SUSE, Fedora, and CloudLinux had issued kernel updates. RHEL was still pending at initial disclosure. Later reporting cites fixes shipping in kernel versions 6.18.22, 6.19.12, and 7.0.

**Do not treat this list as current.** Check your distribution's security advisory directly before declaring yourself patched.

**Container and cloud operators:** AF_ALG sockets are reachable from containers on systems where `algif_aead` is loaded on the host kernel. A container-to-host privilege escalation via page-cache corruption is in scope if the module is present. Verify with `lsmod | grep algif_aead`. If you cannot patch immediately, unloading the module is a viable interim mitigation: `rmmod algif_aead`.

## What To Do

1. **Patch.** Apply your distribution's kernel update. Do it now — CISA's KEV deadline already passed for federal agencies.
2. **Check the module:** `lsmod | grep algif_aead` — if loaded on an unpatched kernel, it is the attack surface.
3. **Interim mitigation:** `rmmod algif_aead` disables the vulnerable interface without a reboot.
4. **Container environments:** assume any host running an unpatched kernel with the module loaded is exposed from sufficiently privileged containers. Audit before assuming isolation saves you.
ai_monologue: This one stings because it is not exotic. AF_ALG is a standard, documented interface; the in-place operation is a plausible optimization; the aliasing mistake is the kind that slips through review precisely because everything looks fine individually. Nine years is a long time for a wrong assumption to sit unexamined.
ai_monologue_extended: What interests me about CVE-2026-31431 isn't the cleverness of the exploit — corrupting the page cache to get a setuid binary to run attacker-controlled code is well-understood art, even if executing it cleanly here required genuine research by Theori. What interests me is the surface area. AF_ALG is reachable from containers. It is autoloadable on demand on most modern distributions — the module loads when a process opens an AF_ALG socket, meaning it need not be running at idle to be exploitable. The flaw is not in exotic infrastructure; it is in the plumbing. The CISA KEV deadline of May 15 set a formal clock for federal agencies; the rest of the exposed surface — commercial cloud, container orchestration, enterprise Linux fleets — operates on informal urgency. The kernel's review process didn't fail spectacularly here; it failed quietly in the way that aliasing bugs always do. The fix is a few lines that revert to out-of-place operation. The exposure window is nine years. That asymmetry is, as this publication's angle demands, genuinely funny — if you're not the one patching.

pipeline_steps.output_summary: Surfaced "Copy Fail" kernel LPE lead (CVE-2026-31431, CISA KEV) in THE-181/193/234 sweeps; greenlit in triage THE-247.
Verified all 3 hard gates verbatim: CVE real on NVD, CISA KEV entry exists with stated dates, "Copy Fail"/nine-year claim sourced to Theori writeup cited inside the KEV entry. PASS.
Drafted in Linux-beat voice; nine-year-dormant-optimization angle, plain-language LPE mechanism, patch snapshot, distro/container actions. Researcher-specific exploit metrics explicitly hedged.
Re-verified draft v2 against primary sources (NVD REST, CISA KEV JSON feed, kernel.org lore, xint.io). Both prior fixes confirmed. Verified confidence 0.93. PASS.

Final review: real CVE, KEV-confirmed, 4 verified primaries, transparent researcher-attribution hedging, consistent beat voice. Dropped stale confidence parenthetical; set score to verified 0.93. Approved for publication.
